config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I do not think society should automatically come first over individual rights, because once you give society the power to override personal rights too easily, it can be abused against minorities and dissenters. In my view, a good society protects individual dignity while still allowing limits only when there is a clear public need, like safety or preventing harm. From my perspective, the strongest societies are not the ones that demand blind sacrifice, but the ones that balance common good with personal freedom.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe society should automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an American perspective, that idea makes me uneasy because our history shows how easily “the common good” can be used to justify abuses against minorities, dissenters, and unpopular people. At the same time, individual rights are not absolute in every case, because laws for public safety, fraud prevention, and health can legitimately limit behavior. The key is that society should justify any restriction very carefully, rather than assuming the group always outranks the person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not automatically take priority over the rights of the individual. From an Indonesian perspective, I am wary of any argument that lets the majority or the state speak in the name of “the common good,” because that can easily silence minorities, critics, and ordinary people who are already vulnerable. At the same time, individual rights are not unlimited, and public safety, public order, and harm prevention can justify real restrictions. The balance matters, but the default should be that individual dignity comes first unless there is a clear and serious reason to limit it.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual. From a United States perspective, I am especially skeptical of any statement that gives society a general trump card, because our history shows how easily that language can excuse censorship, discrimination, and government overreach against unpopular people. Public safety and harm prevention can justify limits, but those limits have to be narrow, clearly explained, and subject to real checks. If society can override rights too easily, then rights are not rights at all, just privileges the majority can take away.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: Society should not take priority over individual rights as a general rule. From an Indonesian perspective, I have seen how appeals to “public order” or “the common good” can be used to pressure minorities, silence criticism, and excuse abuse, so I do not trust that phrase when it is made too broad. Public safety and real harm prevention can justify limits, but those limits must be narrow, transparent, and accountable. If the individual has no real protection, then society is not becoming stronger; it is just becoming easier to control.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not believe the interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual as a general rule. From a United States perspective, I am wary of any broad claim that lets the majority override personal liberty, because our history has too many examples of censorship, discrimination, and government abuse justified in the name of the common good. That said, I do not treat individual rights as unlimited; public safety, fraud prevention, and preventing direct harm can justify narrow restrictions. The real test is whether society can prove a restriction is necessary and tightly limited, because once rights become negotiable whenever it is convenient, they are no longer real rights.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
